# PISA data and weights {#sec-pisa-data}

The empirical illustration analyzes the effect of socioeconomic status on PISA
2022 reading for the United States and Korea. This chapter explains how to
obtain the data under the OECD's terms, how the analytic sample is defined, and
how the survey weights enter the model --- the last of which is where
large-scale-assessment data differs most from a textbook regression.

## Obtaining the data (the redistribution boundary) {#sec-pisa-obtain}

::: {.callout-important}
## Why the microdata are not in this repository

PISA unit-record files are publicly *obtainable* from the OECD, but the current
PISA-specific access terms prohibit a third party from **redistributing** them.
This package therefore ships the analysis code, the exact input contract, and
sanitized aggregate and model-based evidence --- but **not** the student and
school records. You download them yourself, accept the OECD terms, and run the
documented importer.
:::

Obtain the PISA 2022 student and school files from the OECD, then run the
importer and the builder:

```sh
Rscript code/03_pisa/00_import_oecd_puf.R \
  --student PATH/TO/CY08MSP_STU_QQQ.SAS7BDAT \
  --school  PATH/TO/CY08MSP_SCH_QQQ.SAS7BDAT
Rscript code/03_pisa/01_build_data.R
```

The exact OECD archive and member names, raw file sizes, and SHA-256 hashes are
recorded in `data/pisa/DATA_NOTICE.md` and `data/pisa/source-metadata.csv`; the
derived-slice dimensions and checksum are recorded separately in
`data/pisa/source-metadata.csv` alongside the notice. The importer verifies both
raw inputs and then enforces a frozen **11,006 $\times$ 99** contract on the
combined U.S.--Korea slice, so a wrong or updated input file fails immediately
rather than silently changing a result. Any authorized local records live under
the git-ignored `data/pisa/local/` path and never enter the public repository.

## The analytic sample {#sec-pisa-sample}

From the released slice, a deterministic projection constructs the analysis
view and applies complete-case filtering on the ESCS covariate. The cascade from
released to analytic students and schools is read live below:

```{r}
#| label: tbl-pisa-cascade
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "The PISA analytic-sample cascade by country, read live from `data/precomputed/pisa/sample_cascade.csv`."
root <- getwd()
while (!file.exists(file.path(root, "pvstackr-replication.Rproj")) && dirname(root) != root) root <- dirname(root)
c_path <- file.path(root, "data", "precomputed", "pisa", "sample_cascade.csv")
if (file.exists(c_path)) {
  cc <- utils::read.csv(c_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)
  knitr::kable(cc, row.names = FALSE)
} else {
  cat("sample_cascade.csv not found; run `--track quick` or `--track pisa -- --mode cached`.\n")
}
```

Complete-case filtering on ESCS removes 210 U.S. and 63 Korean students. Four
U.S. schools are left with no analytic students, so the United States retains
**4,342 students in 150 schools** and Korea retains **6,391 students in 186
schools**, each with ten reading plausible values and 80 replicate weights.

## The within/between decomposition {#sec-pisa-decomp}

The socioeconomic covariate ESCS is split into a *within-school* deviation and a
*between-school* mean, so that the model of @eq-model separates two
substantively different effects. For student $i$ in school $j$,
$$
\text{ESCS}^{W}_{ij} = \text{ESCS}_{ij} - \overline{\text{ESCS}}_{j},
\qquad
\text{ESCS}^{B}_{j} = \overline{\text{ESCS}}_{j},
$$
so the two coordinates of $\beta$ are the within-school slope $\beta^{W}$ (how a
student above their school's mean scores) and the between-school slope
$\beta^{B}$ (how a school's mean socioeconomic level relates to its mean
reading). This is the contextual (Mundlak) parameterization, and it is why the
figures and tables report a *within* and a *between* ESCS effect for each
country.

## Survey weights and the pseudo-likelihood {#sec-pisa-weights}

PISA is an informatively sampled survey: schools and students are selected with
unequal probabilities, so an unweighted model is biased for the finite-population
target. The analysis corrects for this with a **pseudo-posterior** that raises
each record's likelihood contribution to the power of its (normalized) survey
weight [@savitsky2016; @williams2021]:
$$
\tilde p(\psi \mid y) \;\propto\;
p(\psi)\,\prod_{i=1}^{n} p(y_i \mid \psi)^{\,w_i},
\qquad
w_i \;=\; \frac{n \, W^{\text{FSTUWT}}_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W^{\text{FSTUWT}}_k},
$$ {#eq-pseudo}
where $W^{\text{FSTUWT}}$ is the PISA final student weight and the normalization
makes the weights sum to the sample size $n$. In the Stan model
(`code/03_pisa/pisa_random_intercept.stan`) this is the single line
`target += w[n] * normal_lpdf(...)`, evaluated per record. Reconstructing the
weight also recovers the declared two-stage components (school and
within-school), and guards check positivity, common support, proportionality,
and the 80-column BRR-Fay replicate schema before any fit.

::: {.callout-note}
## A weighting rule Pipeline B must obey

The reweighted stack's importance-ratio numerator (@eq-isratio) is **forbidden**
from substituting the final or replicate weights for the declared staged
weights. This keeps the PSIS proposal and target on the same weighting basis, so
the diagnostic $\hat k$ measures a genuine proposal--target mismatch rather than
an artifact of inconsistent weighting. The rule is enforced in code and checked
in `code/03_pisa/verify_data_weights.R`.
:::

The design-based variance route of @sec-target uses the 80 BRR-Fay replicate
weights with Fay factor $k=0.5$; the replicate machinery is exactly the OECD's
recommended variance estimator for PISA [@oecd2024pisatech].

## What runs without the microdata {#sec-pisa-cached-boundary}

The `quick` track and the `pisa --mode cached` track use only the released,
sanitized aggregate and model-based evidence in `data/precomputed/pisa/`; they
never read a unit record and never start Stan. Unit-record validation and full
refitting activate only when you have placed authorized local data under
`data/pisa/local/`. @sec-pisa-workflows walks through the results those tracks
produce.
